item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately that is not sufficient for it to stay up. The site operates under a CC BY-SA 3.0 license and while there are images like 173's that are labelled as non-commerical usage, those tend to be the exception rather than the rule.

As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Smurfs for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has the potential to be expanded either in a follow up or origins style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mayby they thought they would need more because they have tentacles?

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: Scott Radke, the creator of the first image in the article, has allowed its use as long as it isn't for commercial purposes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Musical little people living in what sounds like an underground world makes me think of Fraggle Rock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though they possess a varying number of tentacle-like appendages on their heads, they have the same physiological needs as human beings.

I don't understand how the clauses of this sentence relate to each other. Why would head-tendrils necessarily suggest a lack of Earth-like physiology?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The instrument bugs feel a little tacked on, but I still really like it! Poor little guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, I really like this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. One of the more underrated skips out there, in my opinion. With a lot of potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How'd I miss this? It's a rare gem of adorable and sad and I want more of the poor lost fellas. The tone is off in the video log though, and "standard humanoid containment cell" seems wrong in this case — the bathroom sink would be above their heads, getting into bed would require a good jump, etc. I'm thinking standard cell scaled to account for their height.

item.title: Poor little guys
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I like SCPs who are tragic instead of hostile. I hope these little guys make it home some day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the concept and interview. I really want one to play for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the tone seems… off in a lot of places. Can't nitpick right now though not enough time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It never fails to horrify.

It never. Ever. Fails.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute and bizarre, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Well, that was weird. I like the geiger smurf description. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that. When I first saw the article, before even reading it, my immediate impression was: "Oh, someone found two SCP fuel pics and tried to write an article about them." Don't get me wrong - it's quite well done. But I'm neutral on it overall. If the picture of the bug-instruments - which, actually, isn't necessary for the article to be comprehended - was removed, I'd be closer to an up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Terribly sorry I didn't get to respond to your PM before this got posted; it's been a busy week or so. That said, I like this. There are a few places where your tone and flow could use some work (e.g. "Testing has revealed these clothes are actually part of their bodies." felt really awkward and could have been incorporated into a previous sentence in a better way); even so, I think it's a good article. While I do appreciate the backstory presented in the interview log, I'm not quite sure it's necessary IMO. The best part of the skip for me was the performance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, so naive. They were chosen to be your God's messangers. Which of course means you worship fear now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh sweet Jesus please no

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If only I could +1 comments…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quit bothering Korean Jesus!

He's busy! With Korean shit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CLOWN MILK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh then you're in for a treat.

Come one, come all, to Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting! See the Man with the Upside-Down Face! Ride Meaty the Meat-Worm! Watch the amazing Mister Uriel as he delights the young and old with his many great talents, that must be seen to be believed! See Madam Rezerta and her amazing Palm Reader! Enjoy our special, one-of-a-kind treat, Black Cotton Candy, available nowhere else on earth but Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Sweet Chinese Jesus. Goddamn that's…wow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lots of tonal issues, especially in the video log, and the speaking of the gnome in the interview is fairly inconsistent. Not sure this has anything going for it beyond that, but that is a pretty good picture up there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't really the place of an agent to be conducting an interview, unless there's a reason, and many times there will be. But the Foundation likely hired (or had on staff) a world-class linguist proficient in umpteen languages to study and analyze 1956 for some time before the interview, and they should be the one conducting it, for fluidity.

Unless the agent had, for whatever reason, been living with theses guys in some other dimension for several years and came to learn their language. Which would be an awesome story.

That said, I haven't even heard of Herman Fuller until now, so I'm kind of lost with this whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, goodness no. Go check out the total number of votes that one has, versus it aggregate vote score.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has to be one of the most hotly contested articles on the website.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To repeat what I said on IRC:

these are Smurfs.

Otherdimensional Giger Smurfs put through a grimdark filter, yes, but still Smurfs. Mushrooms! Headgear!

"We are young, <father/protector> use to say." — PAPA SMURF!

Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, it did seem a little… Reaching at times. At first glance, I was wondering how mutant Teletubby babies and instrument-robot-bugs had anything to do with each other; afterwards, I was trying to figure out why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite GOIs, simply because it's so interesting and diverse what can be done for entertainment. Their performance was well done, as was the article IMO. It was interesting to read, if a little hard to picture at times. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, this really does feel like it's trying too hard to connect the 2 pictures. I can see the strain and it detracts for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Need moar for the Circus. Each and every creation of the Circus is a living, breathing Tom Waits song and it's AMAZING. Those of you wondering which Tom Waits song -these- guys are…

This one. Obviously. Yessssss. Oh yessss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it was begging to to released

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is the agent, someone that's trying to get information from the entity, cutting off SCP-1956-17 and telling it to stop talking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Euclid is the general run of the mill designation for humanoid/sapient SCPs that we don't fully understand in scope and scale of what they can/cannot do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I'm a sucker for weird otherword oddities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably because, as sapient beings, it's impossible to perfectly predict their behavior.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote because I enjoyed this a lot more than the draft, even though the writing in places is more fanciful than foundation. One question though, why is it Euclid?
